step_by_step_reasoning: We start with the internal developers accessing a self-service portal through an ALB, which routes to an EKS-hosted portal. When resources are requested, jobs are placed in an SQS queue. Another EKS cluster running Terraform pulls from this queue and executes infrastructure provisioning. The Terraform code configures Subnets (VPC), shares resources using Resource Access Manager (RAM), and provisions RDS databases across 150+ AWS accounts.

graph.name: Xero: Self-Service Developer Provisioning Platform
graph.notes: Xero built an automated self-service portal enabling over 1,600 developers to provision compliant AWS infrastructure in minutes instead of days, leveraging EKS, Terraform, and AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) to manage 150+ accounts.

nodes.name: Developers
nodes.notes: 1600+ developers accessing the self-service infrastructure portal.

nodes.name: ALB
nodes.notes: Enables secure, VPN-less access to the portal.

nodes.name: EKS (Portal)
nodes.notes: Hosts the frontend and backend of the developer portal.

nodes.name: SQS
nodes.notes: Decouples portal UI from backend provisioning tasks.

nodes.name: EKS (Terraform Runner)
nodes.notes: Runs containerized Terraform jobs to deploy infrastructure.

nodes.name: Terraform
nodes.notes: Infrastructure-as-code tool used to define resources.

nodes.name: Subnets
nodes.notes: Configured across accounts for application deployment.

nodes.name: RAM
nodes.notes: Shares subnets and resources across 150+ AWS accounts.

nodes.name: RDS
nodes.notes: Relational databases provisioned on behalf of developers.

edges.notes: ALB routes traffic to EKS hosting the portal.
Portal places provisioning requests onto SQS.
EKS runners consume provisioning jobs from SQS.
Terraform configuration is applied on EKS.
Utilizes RAM to share subnets across accounts.
